# SDK Coverage Map {#sec-appendix-sdk-map}

This book claims to teach the Playdate Lua SDK, and a claim like that
deserves an audit. This appendix is the audit: every subsection of the
API reference in *Inside Playdate* (§7, SDK 3.0.6) appears in the
tables below, mapped to the chapter — and the chapter section, by its
heading — where the book covers it, with an honest depth rating. Use it
in either direction. Coming from the SDK reference ("what does the book
say about `playdate.pathfinder`?"), scan the leftmost columns for the
namespace and follow the cross-reference. Coming from a gap in your own
game ("I need a scrolling list"), the area names are descriptive enough
to browse.

The depth ratings mean exactly this. **Full**: the book teaches the
area's working core through the chapter's buildable example, including
at least one gotcha — the standard set by the authoring rules. **Introduced**:
the API is explained and situated — what it is, when to reach for it —
with real calls on the page, but the chapter's example does not lean on
it or only part of the area appears. **Mentioned**: named with enough
context to know it exists and where it fits, nothing more. **Not
covered**: absent from the book; every such area gets a sentence at the
end of this appendix saying what it is and where to read about it, so
the map has no blank edges. Ratings describe the area's working core,
not every last function — the fine print after the tables lists the
stray functions inside otherwise-covered areas that the book skips.

## System and runtime

| SDK § | Area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7.1 | Lua enhancements (`+=`, `table` additions, `class()`) | @sec-appendix-lua, "What is added"; demonstrated in @sec-lua-lab | Full |
| 7.2 | System and game metadata (`playdate.metadata`) | @sec-ship, "pdxinfo: the full reference" | Full |
| 7.3 | Game flow (`playdate.update`, `wait`, `stop`, `start`) | @sec-modes, "The update contract" and "The loop-control APIs the skeleton refuses" | Full |
| 7.4 | Game lifecycle callbacks | @sec-tilt, "The rest of the lifecycle"; @sec-save, "Last-chance saves: the lifecycle hooks" | Full |
| 7.5 | System Menu (`getSystemMenu`, menu items, menu image) | @sec-tilt, "The System Menu" | Full |
| 7.6 | Localization (`getSystemLanguage`) | — | Not covered |
| 7.7 | Accessibility (`getReduceFlashing`, `getFlipped`) | — (the master-switch pattern in @sec-juice is where they plug in) | Not covered |
| 7.12 | Device auto lock (`setAutoLockDisabled`) | @sec-tilt, auto-lock warning in "Tying it together" | Full |
| 7.13 | Date & time | @sec-perf (game-clock timing with `getCurrentTimeMilliseconds`); calendar functions tabulated in @sec-appendix-lua | Introduced |
| 7.26 | Power (`getBatteryPercentage`, `getPowerStatus`) | @sec-perf, battery logging aside in "The deeper toolbox" | Mentioned |
| 7.35 | Garbage collection (`setCollectsGarbage`, `setMinimumGCTime`, `setGCScaling`) | @sec-perf, "Garbage: the tax on tables" | Full |

: System and runtime areas. {#tbl-map-system}

## Input

| SDK § | Area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7.8 | Accelerometer | @sec-tilt, "The accelerometer" | Full |
| 7.9 | Buttons (polling, `getButtonState`, button callbacks) | @sec-input, "Six buttons, two questions" and "Everything at once: getButtonState" | Full |
| 7.10 | Crank (position, change, ticks, dock callbacks, crank sounds) | @sec-crank, the whole chapter | Full |
| 7.11 | Input handlers (`inputHandlers.push`/`pop`) | @sec-input, "Polling versus input handlers" | Introduced |
| 7.22 | Keyboard (`playdate.keyboard`) | @sec-tilt, "The keyboard" | Full |

: Input areas. {#tbl-map-input}

## Display, graphics, and UI

| SDK § | Area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7.16 | Display (`setRefreshRate`, `getFPS`) | @sec-modes, "The update contract"; @sec-perf, "The budget, and the meter"; `setRefreshRate(0)` in @sec-harness | Introduced |
| 7.17 | Easing functions | @sec-juice, "The easing catalog" | Full |
| 7.19 | Geometry (point, vector2D, rect, polygon, affineTransform…) | @sec-wireframe, "playdate.geometry: the SDK's vector toolkit" | Introduced |
| 7.32 | UI: `gridview` | @sec-tilt, "A scoreboard with gridview" | Full |
| 7.32 | UI: `crankIndicator` | @sec-crank, "Docked, undocked, and the indicator" | Full |

: Display and UI areas. {#tbl-map-display}

The graphics namespace (§7.20) is the SDK's largest, so it gets its
own table:

| Graphics area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|
| Color, pattern & dither | @sec-dither, "The dither ladder" and "Patterns: rolling your own gray" | Full |
| Drawing primitives | @sec-dither, "The primitive set" | Full |
| Draw modes & modifiers | @sec-dither, "Draw-mode reset discipline"; @sec-images, "Draw modes: one image, many inkings" | Full |
| `image` (load, draw, flips, scale, rotation) | @sec-images, "Two ways to make an image" and "Stamping: draw, flips, scaled, rotated" | Full |
| Offscreen drawing (`pushContext`, `lockFocus`) | @sec-images, "Drawing into an image: pushContext"; put to work in @sec-camera | Full |
| `imagetable` | @sec-images, "Imagetables and the animation loop" | Full |
| `animation.loop` | @sec-images, "Imagetables and the animation loop" | Full |
| `animator` | @sec-juice, "playdate.graphics.animator: values with a shape" | Full |
| `animation.blinker` | @sec-juice, "blinker: the last SDK piece" | Introduced |
| `sprite` (lifecycle, dirty rects, `moveWithCollisions`, groups) | @sec-sprites, the whole chapter | Full |
| `tilemap` | @sec-images, "Tilemaps: many stamps, one object" | Full |
| Text & fonts | @sec-images, "Text as images, and fonts"; @sec-dither, "Text, and white-on-black knockout" | Full |
| Scrolling (`setDrawOffset`) | @sec-camera, "World space, screen space, and the draw offset" | Full |
| Frame buffer (`getDisplayImage`) | @sec-harness, screenshots via `getDisplayImage` | Mentioned |
| `video` | — | Not covered |

: `playdate.graphics` sub-areas (§7.20). {#tbl-map-graphics}

## Sound

The sound namespace (§7.28) is the other giant; the book spends two
chapters inside it and leaves its studio-grade corners alone:

| Sound area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|
| `synth` (waveforms, `playNote`) | @sec-sfx, "One synth, one line" onward | Full |
| Envelopes / ADSR | @sec-sfx, "Shaping a note: ADSR" | Full |
| `lfo` | @sec-sfx, "Wobble: LFOs and modulation" | Full |
| `channel` (volume, per-channel routing) | @sec-sfx, "Channels and the volume knob"; @sec-music, "Ducking: the music channel earns its keep" | Full |
| `sequence` | @sec-music, "Engine one: the SDK sequencer" | Full |
| `track` | @sec-music, "Engine one: the SDK sequencer" | Full |
| `instrument` | @sec-music, "Engine one: the SDK sequencer" | Full |
| `sampleplayer` | @sec-music, "Files: fileplayer and sampleplayer" | Introduced |
| `fileplayer` (streaming, underruns, loop ranges) | @sec-music, "Files: fileplayer and sampleplayer" | Introduced |
| `sample` | — | Not covered |
| `signal` & `controlsignal` | — | Not covered |
| Effects (bitcrusher, ring mod, filters, overdrive, delay line) | — | Not covered |
| Mic input | — | Not covered |
| Audio output & device time | — | Not covered |

: `playdate.sound` sub-areas (§7.28). {#tbl-map-sound}

## Data and files

| SDK § | Area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7.18 | `datastore` | @sec-save, the whole chapter | Full |
| 7.18 | `playdate.file` (open/read/write, filesystem operations) | @sec-save, "Beyond datastores: `json` and `playdate.file`" | Introduced |
| 7.21 | JSON (`json.encode`/`decode` and file variants) | @sec-save, "Beyond datastores"; @sec-appendix-lua, "The `json` global" | Introduced |
| 7.29 | Strings (`CoreLibs/string`: `UUID`, whitespace trims) | — | Not covered |

: Data and file areas. {#tbl-map-data}

## Game-logic helpers

| SDK § | Area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7.23 | Math (`playdate.math.lerp`) | — (@sec-camera hand-rolls `lerp` in one line instead) | Not covered |
| 7.25 | Pathfinding (`pathfinder.graph`, nodes, A*) | @sec-maze, "The SDK's pathfinder: A* for free" | Full |
| 7.30 | Timers (`playdate.timer`, value timers, `updateTimers`) | @sec-juice, "`playdate.timer`: the SDK's clock" | Full |
| 7.31 | Frame timers (`playdate.frameTimer`) | @sec-juice, alongside the timer discussion | Introduced |

: Game-logic helper areas. {#tbl-map-logic}

## Development and tooling

| SDK § | Area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7.14 | Debugging (`drawFPS`, `print`) | @sec-perf, "The budget, and the meter"; @sec-lua-lab, "print still works" | Introduced |
| 7.15 | Profiling (`sample()`, `getStats`, the Simulator's tools) | @sec-perf, "The deeper toolbox" and "Measuring the drawing API" | Full |
| 7.27 | Simulator-only (`playdate.simulator.writeToFile` and friends) | @sec-harness, "writeToFile, not datastore.writeImage"; @sec-appendix-harness | Introduced |

: Development and tooling areas. {#tbl-map-tooling}

## Connectivity

| SDK § | Area | Where in the book | Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7.24 | Networking (HTTP, TCP) | — | Not covered |
| 7.33 | Serial communication | — | Not covered |
| 7.34 | Playdate Mirror | — | Not covered |

: Connectivity areas. {#tbl-map-connectivity}

## The tally

Sixty-four areas: **37 Full, 12 Introduced, 2 Mentioned, 13 Not
covered.** Everything a single-player game running on the device
touches — graphics, sound, input, timers, persistence, pathfinding,
the lifecycle — is taught at Full or Introduced depth. The Not covered
column clusters at the edges: connectivity, recorded-media plumbing,
and single-function conveniences. Here is what each one is, so you
know whether you need it.

**Localization** (§7.6) is one function: `playdate.getSystemLanguage()`
returns whether the console is set to English or Japanese, so a
bilingual game can pick its strings. **Accessibility** (§7.7) is two
reads — `getReduceFlashing()` and `getFlipped()` report the user's
system settings; a game with flash-heavy effects should check the
former and route it into a master switch like the one @sec-juice
builds. **Video** (§7.20) plays pre-rendered `.pdv` files through
`playdate.graphics.video` — for cutscenes, and rarely worth the
storage in a 1-bit game. In sound, **`sample`** (§7.28) is the raw
audio-buffer object beneath `sampleplayer` (slicing, saving, loading);
**`signal` and `controlsignal`** are modulation sources that automate
synth parameters from sequencer tracks; the **effects** family
(bitcrusher, ring modulator, one- and two-pole filters, overdrive,
delay line) attaches DSP to channels; **mic input** records from the
built-in or headset microphone; and **audio output** covers headphone
detection and the audio device clock. **Strings** (§7.29,
`CoreLibs/string`) adds `UUID()` and three whitespace-trimming
functions to standard Lua's `string`. **Math** (§7.23) is exactly one
function, `playdate.math.lerp` — this book writes
`a + (b - a) * t` where it needs it. **Networking** (§7.24, OS 2.7+)
brings HTTP and TCP, up to four connections, behind a user-consent
prompt; **serial communication** (§7.33) lets a game receive `msg`
commands over USB; and **Playdate Mirror** (§7.34) provides callbacks
so a game can lighten its load while the Mirror app streams it to a
computer. For all of these, the numbered section of *Inside Playdate*
in the left column is the definitive reference — and after eighteen
chapters of this book's habits, none of them will read as foreign.

Fine print on the partially covered rows: within areas rated Full or
Introduced, the book does not use `playdate.apiVersion()` (§7.2),
the calendar half of date & time (`getTime`, `getGMTTime`,
epoch conversions — tabulated but not exercised in
@sec-appendix-lua), the display transforms `setScale`, `setInverted`,
`setMosaic`, `setFlipped`, and `setOffset` (§7.16), or the console
helpers `printTable` and `where()` (§7.14). Each behaves exactly as
its reference entry says, and none hides a trap the book's covered
neighbors have not already taught you to expect.
